publicationStmt.p: Transcribed from Bodleian Library, Lovelace Byron collection, Box 166, folios 9-13. Transcription by Lisa Gold (2022). TEI conversion for Sextant pipeline, 2026.

div.p: Tuesday

Dearest W. All is still uncertain; & I only fear it may be a vexation to you. It is certainly quite necessary for the Hen to be now in Town, for advice; & it is also better for her to have me with her I think, instead of
being alone, or having E--. She seems to find in me both comfort & amusement.

There has just been a consultation between Locock & a Dr Williams about her. The latter is a celebrated stethoscopist; & it appears that he has arrived at some important conclusions respecting her. But as yet their opinion is not
fully disclosed to us.
I expect Locock will be here this evening again, & that something more definite will be then settled, than has been hitherto. She is decidely better for his measures already.
I am much distressed to see how she suffers from her affliction, & anxiety, & I fear I must say in some degree disappointment about
E--, whom she certainly loves as her own child.
Poor Hen! Everyone to whom she is generous, seems to take advantage of it.--
Miss Jane is already improving a little. The more I see, the more I like her. But I think you will be disappointed at first perhaps; because she is in such very shabby & miserable condition, &
shows all that is least prepossessing & attractive about her.

She is in my opinion beautiful in her prints & make; & so gentle, poor thing. Altho' I hear she has begun her kicking when mounted. But it seems to be all play & spirits. The saddle- -horses are of great use to me. I only hope you do not want
them. I am vexed to think you have only that common little half-bred hack; so unfit for you to appear upon. Perhaps however you do not ride much, now I am away.--
Now for what I wanted to say about your teaching Byron French or Latin. You, dear William, have no experience in

teaching children; & I am persuaded it could only disgust both you & poor B--. You do not know what a child can do; & would take for idleness or inattention what is only inherent to his years. Neither have you any idea how to make so dull a thing as the acquisition of language, engaging to 6 years old.
I also think it very unadvisable that a parent should ever be a direct teacher.
You can do far more for B--, believe me, in taking him out of doors, & conversing with him on the objects around him; or in assisting his drawings.
Forgive my writing you this dissertation.--
Do ask Stephen where he took my Perzian ink-stand. Instead of
mending it, they have sent me back a smaller one, which don't do, & acts very badly. I am quite vexed about it. It is a great shame.
